terms.definition: A platform for packaging an application and everything it needs into a portable, runnable unit
terms.example: e.g. Docker lets the same coffee-supplier app run identically on DockerHost, a laptop, or a teammate's machine

terms.definition: A read-only template, built from a Dockerfile, that contains an application's code, runtime, and dependencies
terms.example: e.g. the node_app image built with `docker build --tag node_app .`

terms.definition: A running instance of a Docker image
terms.example: e.g. `node_app_1`, created by running `docker run` against the node_app image

terms.definition: A text file containing step-by-step instructions Docker uses to build an image
terms.example: e.g. a Dockerfile that starts `FROM node:11-alpine` and ends with `CMD ["npm", "run", "start"]`

terms.definition: The background service that builds images and runs, stops, and manages containers on a host machine
terms.example: e.g. the Docker Engine installed on DockerHost in Task 3

terms.definition: The starting image a Dockerfile builds on top of, declared with the FROM instruction
terms.example: e.g. `node:11-alpine` is the base image for the node_app Dockerfile

terms.definition: A minimal Linux distribution commonly used as a Docker base image because of its small size
terms.example: e.g. `node:11-alpine` is far smaller than a full `node:11` image

terms.definition: One cached filesystem change produced by a single Dockerfile instruction, stacked to form the final image
terms.example: e.g. each of RUN, COPY, and EXPOSE in a Dockerfile creates its own layer

terms.definition: A human-readable label attached to a specific version of an image, such as latest or v1
terms.example: e.g. `docker tag node_app:latest node_app:production`

terms.definition: An immutable, content-based fingerprint (SHA-256 hash) that uniquely identifies an exact image, unlike a tag which can be reassigned
terms.example: e.g. the digest returned by `aws ecr describe-images` after pushing to Amazon ECR

terms.definition: A managed AWS service for storing, managing, and sharing Docker container images
terms.example: e.g. the node-app repository created in Task 8 of this lab

terms.definition: A named collection inside Amazon ECR that stores one or more versions (tags) of a Docker image
terms.example: e.g. the `node-app` repository created with `aws ecr create-repository`

terms.definition: A service that stores and distributes Docker images, whether public (Docker Hub) or private (Amazon ECR)
terms.example: e.g. Docker pulls the `node:11-alpine` base image from Docker Hub, a public container registry

terms.definition: The command that reads a Dockerfile and produces a Docker image
terms.example: e.g. `docker build --tag node_app .`

terms.definition: The command that creates and starts a container from a Docker image
terms.example: e.g. `docker run -d --name node_app_1 -p 3000:3000 node_app`

terms.definition: A command that runs a new process, such as a shell, inside an already-running container
terms.example: e.g. `docker exec -i mysql_1 mysql -u root -prootpw`

terms.definition: A command that lists currently running containers (docker container ls is the newer equivalent)
terms.example: e.g. running `docker ps` to find the container ID of mysql_1

terms.definition: A command that returns detailed JSON configuration and runtime information about an image or container
terms.example: e.g. `docker inspect mysql_1` to find its internal IP address

terms.definition: A command that prints the standard output/error logs generated by a container
terms.example: e.g. `docker logs node_app_1` to check for startup errors

terms.definition: A command that assigns a new name/tag to an existing local image, without duplicating its layers
terms.example: e.g. `docker tag node_app:latest <registry-id.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/node-app:latest`

terms.definition: A command that uploads a tagged local image to a remote container registry
terms.example: e.g. `docker push <registry-id.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/node-app:latest`

terms.definition: A docker run flag that starts a container in the background instead of attaching your terminal to it
terms.example: e.g. `docker run -d --name node_app_1 ...`

terms.definition: A docker run flag that connects a port on the host machine to a port inside the container
terms.example: e.g. `-p 3000:3000` maps host port 3000 to container port 3000

terms.definition: A Dockerfile instruction that documents which port the container listens on (does not itself publish the port)
terms.example: e.g. `EXPOSE 3000` in the node_app Dockerfile

terms.definition: A Dockerfile instruction that sets the working directory inside the image for subsequent instructions
terms.example: e.g. `WORKDIR /usr/src/app`

terms.definition: A key-value setting passed into a container at runtime, often used to configure behavior without changing the image
terms.example: e.g. `-e APP_DB_HOST="<ip-address"` telling the app where to find its database

terms.definition: Docker's default private network that lets containers on the same host communicate using internal IP addresses
terms.example: e.g. node_app_1 and mysql_1 communicating over the default bridge network in Task 7

terms.definition: A MySQL/MariaDB command-line utility that exports a database's schema and data into a portable SQL file
terms.example: e.g. `mysqldump -P 3306 -h <ip -u nodeapp -p --databases COFFEE  my_sql.sql`

terms.definition: AWS's virtual server (compute instance) service, used in this lab to host the app, the database, and the container host
terms.example: e.g. the AppServerNode, MysqlServerNode, and DockerHost instances you launch yourself

terms.definition: An identity in AWS Identity and Access Management representing a person or workload, with its own console password and/or access keys
terms.example: e.g. the docker-lab-admin user you created in Task 1 to avoid working as the root user

terms.definition: An AWS identity with permissions that can be assumed temporarily by a service, application, or user, instead of using long-lived credentials
terms.example: e.g. docker-lab-ecr-role, assumed automatically by DockerHost to authenticate to Amazon ECR

terms.definition: A container for an IAM role that lets an EC2 instance assume that role automatically at launch
terms.example: e.g. the instance profile attached to DockerHost in Task 3, generated automatically when you created docker-lab-ecr-role

terms.definition: A virtual, stateful firewall attached to EC2 instances that controls inbound and outbound traffic by rule
terms.example: e.g. docker-lab-sg, allowing SSH, port 80, port 3000, and self-referencing port 3306 traffic

terms.definition: A public/private key combination used to securely authenticate SSH connections to an EC2 instance, without a password
terms.example: e.g. docker-lab-key.pem, used to SSH into all three EC2 instances in this lab

terms.definition: A browser-based SSH connection method built into the EC2 console, usable without a downloaded key pair
terms.example: e.g. connecting to MysqlServerNode from the EC2 console instead of a local terminal

terms.definition: A set of usage allowances (like 750 EC2 instance-hours/month) available at no cost, typically for a new AWS account's first 12 months
terms.example: e.g. three t3.micro instances running for this lab's duration typically stay within Free Tier limits

terms.definition: An AWS CLI command that returns the identity (user, role, or account) currently being used to make API calls
terms.example: e.g. run on DockerHost in Task 3 to confirm it's authenticating as docker-lab-ecr-role, not a stored access key
